skill.name: risk-metrics-calculation
skill.description: Calculate portfolio risk metrics including VaR, CVaR, Sharpe, Sortino, and drawdown analysis. Use when measuring portfolio risk, implementing risk limits, or building risk monitoring systems.
skill.summary: Comprehensive risk measurement toolkit for portfolio management with Value at Risk, Expected Shortfall, and drawdown analysis.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All static analysis findings were determined to be false positives. The reported 'backtick execution' patterns are markdown code fence delimiters in documentation, not shell commands. The 'hardcoded URLs' are educational resource links (Amazon book references). The skill contains pure Python code for financial risk calculations with no command execution, network operations, or dangerous patterns.

content.user_title: Calculate Portfolio Risk Metrics and Analysis
content.value_statement: Financial professionals need accurate risk measurement to protect capital and optimize returns. This skill provides comprehensive risk metrics including VaR, CVaR, Sharpe ratio, Sortino ratio, and drawdown analysis for informed investment decisions.
actual_capabilities: Calculate Value at Risk (VaR) using historical, parametric, and Cornish-Fisher methods
Compute Expected Shortfall (CVaR) for tail risk assessment
Generate risk-adjusted return metrics: Sharpe, Sortino, Calmar, and Omega ratios
Perform drawdown analysis including maximum drawdown and duration statistics
Calculate rolling risk metrics for time-varying risk assessment
Conduct stress testing with historical scenarios and Monte Carlo simulations
limitations: Requires historical return data as input; does not fetch market data automatically
Statistical methods assume sufficient data history for reliable estimates
Monte Carlo simulations use normal distribution assumptions which may underestimate fat tails
Risk parity optimization requires scipy and may be computationally intensive for large portfolios

use_cases.title: Portfolio Risk Dashboard
use_cases.description: Build comprehensive risk monitoring dashboards that track VaR, volatility, and drawdowns in real-time for investment committees and regulatory reporting.
use_cases.target_user: Risk managers at asset management firms

use_cases.title: Backtesting Strategy Risk
use_cases.description: Evaluate trading strategy risk profiles by calculating Sharpe ratios, maximum drawdowns, and stress test performance during historical crisis periods.
use_cases.target_user: Quantitative researchers developing algorithmic trading strategies

use_cases.title: Position Sizing Framework
use_cases.description: Implement risk-based position sizing by analyzing marginal risk contributions and component risk to optimize portfolio allocation.
use_cases.target_user: Portfolio managers constructing diversified investment portfolios

prompt_templates.title: Basic Risk Summary
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a series of daily portfolio returns. Calculate the key risk metrics including volatility, Sharpe ratio, maximum drawdown, and Value at Risk at 95% confidence. Here are my returns: [paste returns data]
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: Quick risk assessment for a portfolio

prompt_templates.title: Comparative Risk Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the risk profiles of two investment strategies. For each, calculate annualized volatility, Sortino ratio, CVaR at 99% confidence, and Calmar ratio. Identify which strategy has better risk-adjusted returns and lower tail risk. Strategy A returns: [data]. Strategy B returns: [data].

prompt_templates.title: Rolling Risk Regime Detection
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze how portfolio risk has evolved over time using rolling 63-day windows. Calculate rolling volatility, rolling Sharpe, and rolling maximum drawdown. Identify distinct volatility regimes and flag periods where risk exceeded acceptable thresholds.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: Time-varying risk analysis for dynamic allocation

prompt_templates.title: Stress Testing and Scenario Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Conduct comprehensive stress testing on my portfolio. Run historical stress tests for the 2008 financial crisis, 2020 COVID crash, and 2022 rate hikes. Also perform Monte Carlo simulation with 10,000 paths over 21 days with 2x normal volatility. Report worst-case losses and probability of exceeding 10% loss.
prompt_templates.scenario: Expert: Full stress testing framework for risk committees

output_examples.input: Calculate risk metrics for a portfolio with annual return of 12%, volatility of 15%, and maximum drawdown of -22%
output_examples.output:
Risk Summary:
- Annualized Volatility: 15.0%
- Sharpe Ratio (assuming 2% risk-free): 0.67
- Maximum Drawdown: -22.0%
- Value at Risk (95%): -2.1%
- Conditional VaR (95%): -3.4%
- Calmar Ratio: 0.55

Assessment: Moderate risk profile with acceptable risk-adjusted returns. Maximum drawdown exceeds typical 20% threshold for conservative portfolios.

output_examples.input: Compare rolling volatility across different market regimes for a 10-year return series
output_examples.output:
Rolling Volatility Analysis (63-day window):

- Low Volatility Regime (<10%): 45% of periods
- Normal Regime (10-20%): 38% of periods
- High Volatility Regime (>20%): 17% of periods

Key Observations:
- Current volatility: 18.5% (Normal regime)
- Peak volatility: 42.3% during March 2020
- Average volatility: 14.2%
- Volatility clustering detected: High persistence (0.82 autocorrelation)

Recommendation: Consider reducing exposure during sustained high volatility periods.

best_practices: Use multiple complementary metrics rather than relying on a single measure - VaR alone underestimates tail risk, so always pair with CVaR
Apply rolling window analysis to capture time-varying risk - static metrics miss regime changes and evolving market conditions
Conduct both historical and hypothetical stress tests - past crises inform but do not predict future tail events
anti_patterns: Assuming normal distribution for returns - financial returns exhibit fat tails and skewness that parametric methods may underestimate
Using short lookback periods - risk estimates from less than 3 years of data may miss important regime changes and crisis periods
Ignoring correlation dynamics - correlations typically increase during stress periods, reducing diversification benefits when most needed

faq.question: What is the difference between VaR and CVaR?
faq.answer: Value at Risk (VaR) tells you the maximum loss at a given confidence level (e.g., 95%). Conditional VaR (CVaR), also called Expected Shortfall, tells you the average loss when losses exceed the VaR threshold. CVaR is more conservative and captures tail risk that VaR misses.

faq.question: How much historical data do I need for reliable risk estimates?
faq.answer: Minimum 252 trading days (1 year) for basic metrics. For robust estimates, use 3-5 years of daily data. Stress testing and regime analysis benefit from 10+ years to capture multiple market cycles including crisis periods.

faq.question: Why is my Sharpe ratio different from other calculations?
faq.answer: Differences arise from: annualization factor (252 vs 250 trading days), risk-free rate assumption, return frequency (daily vs monthly), and whether returns are arithmetic or geometric. This skill uses 252 trading days with arithmetic returns.

faq.question: Can I use this for cryptocurrency portfolios?
faq.answer: Yes, the mathematical methods apply to any return series. However, crypto assets exhibit higher volatility and different distributional properties. Consider using higher confidence levels (99% vs 95%) and shorter lookback periods to capture recent regime behavior.

faq.question: What confidence level should I use for VaR?
faq.answer: 95% is standard for general risk reporting. Use 99% for regulatory capital, stress testing, or when tail risk is a primary concern. Higher confidence provides more conservative estimates but requires more data for statistical significance.

faq.question: How do I interpret the Calmar ratio?
faq.answer: Calmar ratio = Annualized Return / Maximum Drawdown. A Calmar above 1.0 indicates returns exceeded maximum drawdown (favorable). Below 0.5 suggests poor risk-adjusted performance. Useful for comparing strategies with similar objectives but different drawdown profiles.
